1 .7 GROUP ACTIONS

In this section we introduce the precise definition of a group acting on a set and present some examples. Group actions will be a powerful tool which we shall use both for proving theorems for abstract groups and for unravelling the structure of specific examples. Moreover, the concept of an "action" is a theme which will recur throughout the text as a method for studying an algebraic object by seeing how it can act on other structures.

Definition. A group action of a group $G$ on a set A is a map from $G \times A$ to A (written as ${ \pmb g } \cdot { \pmb a }$ , for all $g \in G$ and $a \in A .$ ) satisfying the following properties:

list_items: (1) $g _ { 1 } \cdot ( g _ { 2 } \cdot a ) = ( g _ { 1 } g _ { 2 } ) \cdot a .$ , for all $\qquad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad$ , $a \in A$ , and
(2) $\mathbf { 1 } { \cdot } a = a$ , for all $a \in A$ .

text: Sec. 1 .7 Group Actions

We shall immediately become less formal and say $G$ is a group acting on a set A . The expression $g \cdot a$ will usually b e written simply a s ${ \pmb g } { \pmb a }$ when there is no danger of confusing this map with, say, the group operation (remember, · is not a binary operation and ${ \pmb g } { \pmb a }$ is always a member of A). Note that on the left hand side of the equation in property (1) $g _ { 2 } \cdot a$ is an element of A so it makes sense to act on this by $_ { g _ { 1 } }$ • On the right hand side of this equation the product $( g _ { 1 } g _ { 2 } )$ is taken in $G$ and the resulting group element acts on the set element $\pmb { a }$ .
Before giving some examples of group actions we make some observations. Let the group $\pmb { G }$ act on the set A. For each fixed $g \in G$ we get a map $\sigma _ { g }$ defined by
We prove two important facts:

list_items: (i) for each fixed $g \in G$ , $\sigma _ { g }$ is a permutation of A, and
(ii) the map from $\pmb { G }$ to $S _ { A }$ defined by $g \mapsto \sigma _ { g }$ is a homomorphism.

text: To see that $\sigma _ { g }$ is a permutation of A we show that as a set map from A to A it has a 2-sided inverse, namely $\sigma _ { g ^ { - 1 } }$ (it is then a permutation by Proposition 1 of Section 0. 1 ). For all $a \in A$

This proves $\sigma _ { g ^ { - 1 } } \circ \sigma _ { g }$ is the identity map from A to A. Since g was arbitrary, we may interchange the roles of $_ { g }$ and $g ^ { - 1 }$ to obtain $\sigma _ { g } \circ \sigma _ { g ^ { - 1 } }$ is also the identity map on A . Thus $\sigma _ { g }$ has a 2-sided inverse, hence is a permutation of A.

To check assertion (ii) above let $\varphi : G \to S _ { A }$ be defined by $\varphi ( \pmb { \mathscr { g } } ) = \pmb { \sigma } _ { \pmb { \mathscr { g } } }$ . Note that part (i) shows that $\sigma _ { g }$ is indeed an element of $s _ { A }$ . To see that $\varphi$ is a homomorphism we must prove $\varphi ( g _ { 1 } g _ { 2 } ) = \varphi ( g _ { 1 } ) \circ \varphi ( g _ { 2 } )$ (recall that $s _ { A }$ is a group under function composition). The permutations $\varphi ( g _ { 1 } g _ { 2 } )$ and $\varphi ( g _ { 1 } ) \circ \varphi ( g _ { 2 } )$ are equal if and only if their values agree on every element $a \in A$ . For all $a \in A$
This proves assertion (ii) above.

Intuitively, a group action of $\pmb { G }$ on a set A just means that every element $_ { g }$ in $\pmb { G }$ acts as a permutation on A in a manner consistent with the group operations in $G \mathrm { : }$ ; assertions (i) and (ii) above make this precise. The homomorphism from $G$ to $S _ { A }$ given above is
Chap. 1 I ntroduction to Groups

called the permutation representation associated to the given action. It is easy to see that this process is reversible in the sense that if $\varphi : G \to S _ { A }$ is any homomorphism from a group $G$ to the symmetric group on a set A, then the map from $G \times A$ to A defined by

$$
g \cdot a = \varphi (g) (a) \quad \text {f o r a l l} g \in G, \text {a n d a l l} a \in A
$$

satisfies the properties of a group action of $\pmb { G }$ on A. Thus actions of a group $G$ on a set A and the homomorphisms from $G$ into the symmetric group $s _ { A }$ are in bijective correspondence (i.e., are essentially the same notion, phrased in different terminology).

We should also note that the definition of an action might have been more precisely named a left action since the group elements appear on the left of the set elements. We could similarly define the notion of a right action.
Examples
Let $\pmb { G }$ be a group and A a nonempty set. In each of the following examples the check of properties (1) and (2) of an action are left as exercises.

(1) Let $g a = a$ , for all $g \in G$ , $a \in A$ . Properties ( 1 ) and (2) of a group action follow immediately. This action is called the trivial action and $\pmb { G }$ is said to act trivially on A. Note that distinct elements of $\pmb { G }$ induce the same permutation on A (in this case the identity permU1ation). The associated permutation representation $G \to S _ { A }$ is the trivial homomorphism which maps every element of $\pmb { G }$ to the identity.

If $\pmb { G }$ acts on a set $\pmb { B }$ and distinct elements of $\pmb { G }$ induce distinct permutations of $B _ { i }$ , the action is said to be faithful. A faithful action is therefore one in which the associated permutation representation is injective.

The kernel of the action of $\pmb { G }$ on $\pmb { B }$ is defined to be $\{ g \in G \mid g b = b$ for all $b \in B \}$ } , namely the elements of $\pmb { G }$ which fix all the elements of $\pmb { B }$ . For the trivial action, the kernel of the action is all of $\pmb { G }$ and this action is not faithful when $| G | > 1$ .

(2) The axioms for a vector space $V$ over a field $F$ include the two axioms that the multiplicative group $\pmb { F } ^ { \times }$ act on the set V. Thus vector spaces are familiar examples of actions of multiplicative groups of fields where there is even more structure (in particular, $V$ must be an abelian group) which can be exploited. In the special case when $V = \mathbb { R } ^ { n }$ and $F = \mathbb { R }$ the action is specified by

$$
\alpha \left(r _ {1}, r _ {2}, \dots , r _ {n}\right) = \left(\alpha r _ {1}, \alpha r _ {2}, \dots , \alpha r _ {n}\right)
$$

for all ${ \pmb { \alpha } } \in \mathbb { R } .$ $, ( r _ { 1 } , r _ { 2 } , \ldots , r _ { n } ) \in \mathbb { R } ^ { n }$ , where $\pmb { \alpha } r _ { i }$ is just multiplication of two real numbers.

(3) For any nonempty set A the symmetric group $s _ { A }$ acts on A by $\sigma \cdot a = \sigma ( a )$ , for all $\sigma \in S _ { A }$ , $a \in A$ . The associated permutation representation is the identity map from $s _ { A }$ to itself.

(4) If we fix a labelling of the vertices of a regular n-gon, each element $\pmb { \alpha }$ of $D _ { 2 n }$ gives rise to a permutation $\pmb { \sigma } _ { \pmb { \alpha } }$ of $\{ 1 , 2 , \ldots , n \}$ by the way the symmetry $\pmb { \alpha }$ permutes the corresponding vertices. The map of $D _ { 2 n } \times \{ 1 , 2 , \ldots , n \}$ onto $\left\{ 1 , 2 , \ldots , n \right\}$ defined by $( \alpha , i ) \to \sigma _ { \alpha } ( i )$ defines a group action of $D _ { 2 n }$ on $\left\{ 1 , 2 , \ldots , n \right\}$ . In keeping with our notation for group actions we can now dispense with the formal and cumbersome notation $\sigma _ { \alpha } ( i )$ and write ai in its place. Note that this action is faithful: distinct symmetries of a regular n-gon induce distinct permutations of the vertices.

When ${ \pmb n } = 3$ the action of ${ D } _ { 6 }$ on the three (labelled) vertices of a triangle gives an injective homomorphism from $D _ { 6 }$ to $s _ { 3 }$ . Since these groups have the same order, this map must also be smjective, i.e., is an isomorphism: $D _ { 6 } \cong S _ { 3 }$ . This is another
Sec. 1 .7 Grou p Actions

proof of the same fact we established via generators and relations in the preceding section. Geometrically it says that any permutation of the vertices of a triangle is a symmetry. The analogous statement is not true for any $\pmb { n }$ -gon with $n \geq 4$ Gust by order considerations we cannot have $D _ { 2 n }$ isomorphic to $s _ { n }$ for any $n \geq 4$ ) .

(5) Let G be any group and let $A = G .$ . Define a map from $G \times A$ to A by $g \cdot a = g a$ , for each $g \in G$ and $a \in A$ , where ga on the right hand side is the product of $_ { g }$ and $\pmb { a }$ in the group $\pmb { G }$ . This gives a group action of $\pmb { G }$ on itself, where each (fixed) $g \in G$ permutes the elements of $\pmb { G }$ by left multiplication:

$$
g: a \mapsto g a \quad \text {f o r a l l} a \in G
$$

(or, if $\pmb { G }$ is written additively, we get $\alpha \mapsto g + \alpha$ and call this left translation). This action is called the left regular action of $\pmb { G }$ on itself. By the cancellation laws, this action is faithful (check this).
Other examples of actions are given in the exercises.

text: Sec 1 . 7 Group Actions
CHAPTER 2
Su bgro u ps
2. 1 DEFIN ITION AND EXAMPLES

One basic method for unravelling the structure of any mathematical object which is defined by a set of axioms is to study subsets of that object which also satisfy the same axioms. We begin this program by discussing subgroups of a group. A second basic method for unravelling structure is to study quotients of an object; the notion of a quotient group, which is a way (roughly speaking) of collapsing one group onto a smaller group, will be dealt with in the next chapter. Both of these themes will recur throughout the text as we study subgroups and quotient groups of a group, subrings and quotient rings of a ring, subspaces and quotient spaces of a vector space, etc.

Definition. Let $G$ be a group. The subset $H$ of $\pmb { G }$ is a subgroup of $G$ if $H$ is nonempty and $H$ is closed under products and inverses (i.e., $x , y \in H$ implies ${ \boldsymbol { x } } ^ { - 1 } \in H$ and $x y \in H$ ). If $H$ is a subgroup of $G$ we shall write $H \leq G$ .

Subgroups of $\pmb { G }$ are just subsets o f $\pmb { G }$ which are themselves groups with respect to the operation defined in $G ,$ , i.e., the binary operation on $G$ restricts to give a binary operation on $H$ which is associative, has an identity in $H$ , and has inverses in $H$ for all the elements of $H$ .

When we say that $H$ is a subgroup of $G$ we shall always mean that the operation for the group $H$ is the operation on $G$ restricted to $H$ (in general it is possible that the subset $H$ has the structure of a group with respect to some operation other than the operation on $\pmb { G }$ restricted to $H _ { ; }$ , cf. Example 5(a) following). As we have been doing for functions restricted to a subset, we shall denote the operation for $G$ and the operation for the subgroup $H$ by the same symbol. If $H \leq G$ and $H \neq G$ we shall write $H < G$ to emphasize that the containment is proper.

If $H$ is a subgroup of $\pmb { G }$ then, since the operation for $H$ is the operation for $\pmb { G }$ restricted to $H$ , any equation in the subgroup $H$ may also be viewed as an equation in the group $G$ . Thus the cancellation laws for $\pmb { G }$ imply that the identity for $H$ is the same as the identity of $\pmb { G }$ (in particular, every subgroup must contain 1 , the identity of $\pmb { G }$ ) and the inverse of an element $x$ in $H$ is the same as the inverse of $x$ when considered as an element of $\pmb { G }$ (so the notation $x ^ { - 1 }$ is unambiguous).
Examples
(1) $\mathbb { Z } \leq \mathbb { Q }$ and $\mathbb { Q } \leq \mathbb { R }$ with the operation of addition.
(2) Any group $\pmb { G }$ has two subgroups: $H = G$ and $H = \{ 1 \}$ ; the latter is called the trivial subgroup and will henceforth be denoted by 1.

(3) If $G = D _ { 2 n }$ is the dihedral group of order $_ { 2 n }$ , let $H$ be $\{ 1 , r , r ^ { 2 } , \ldots , r ^ { n - 1 } \}$ , the set of all rotations in $\pmb { G }$ . Since the product of two rotations is again a rotation and the inverse of a rotation is also a rotation it follows that $\pmb { H }$ is a subgroup of $D _ { 2 n }$ of order n.
( 4) The set of even integers is a subgroup of the group of all integers under addition.
(5) Some examples of subsets which are not subgroups:

(a) $\mathbb { Q } - \{ \mathbf { 0 } \}$ under multiplication is not a subgroup of $\mathbb { R }$ under addition even though both are groups and $\mathbb { Q } - \{ \mathbf { 0 } \}$ is a subset of $\mathbb { R } ;$ ; the operation of multiplication on $\mathbb { Q } - \{ \mathbf { 0 } \}$ is not the restriction of the operation of addition on $\mathbb { R }$ .

(b) $\mathbb { Z } ^ { + }$ (under addition) is not a subgroup of $\mathbb { Z }$ (under addition) because although $\mathbb { Z } ^ { + }$ is closed under $+ ,$ , it does not contain the identity, 0, of $\mathbb { Z }$ and although each $x \in \mathbb { Z } ^ { + }$ has an additive inverse, $- { \pmb x } ,$ , in $\mathbb { Z } , - x \notin \mathbb { Z } ^ { + }$ , i .e., $\mathbb { Z } ^ { + }$ is not closed under the operation of taking inverses (in particular, $\mathbb { Z } ^ { + }$ is not a group under addition). For analogous reasons, $( \mathbb { Z } - \{ { \bf 0 } \} , { \bf \times } )$ is not a subgroup of $( \mathbb { Q } - \{ 0 \} , \times )$ .
(c) $D _ { 6 }$ is not a subgroup of $D _ { 8 }$ since the former is not even a subset of the latter.

(6) The relation "is a subgroup of' is transitive: if $\pmb { H }$ is a subgroup of a group $\pmb { G }$ and $\pmb { K }$ is a subgroup of $H .$ , then $\pmb { K }$ is also a subgroup of $\pmb { G }$ .

As we saw in Chapter 1, even for easy examples checking that all the group axioms (especially the associative law) hold for any given binary operation can be tedious at best. Once we know that we have a group, however, checking that a subset of it is (or is not) a subgroup is a much easier task, since all we need to check is closure under multiplication and under taking inverses. The next proposition shows that these can be amalgamated into a single test and also shows that for finite groups it suffices to check for closure under multiplication.
Proposition 1. (The Subgroup Criterion) A subset $\pmb { H }$ of a group $G$ is a subgroup if and only if

list_items: (1) $H \neq \emptyset$ , and
(2) for all $x , y \in H , x y ^ { - 1 } \in H .$

text: Furthermore, if $H$ is finite, then it suffices to check that $\pmb { H }$ is nonempty and closed under multiplication.

Proof" If $\pmb { H }$ is a subgroup of $G$ , then certainly ( 1 ) and (2) hold because $\pmb { H }$ contains the identity of $G$ and the inverse of each of its elements and because $\pmb { H }$ is closed under multiplication.

It remains to show conversely that if $H$ satisfies both ( 1 ) and (2), then $H \leq G$ . Let $x$ be any element in $\pmb { H }$ (such $_ { x }$ exists by property ( 1 )). Let ${ \boldsymbol { y } } = { \boldsymbol { x } }$ and apply property (2) to deduce that $1 = x x ^ { - 1 } \in H ,$ , so $H$ contains the identity of $G$ . Then, again by (2), since $\pmb { H }$ contains 1 and $_ { x }$ , $\pmb { H }$ contains the element $1 x ^ { - 1 }$ , i . e . , ${ \boldsymbol { x } } ^ { - 1 } \in H$ and $\pmb { H }$ is closed under taking inverses. Finally, if $x$ and $y$ are any two elements of $\pmb { H }$ , then $\pmb { H }$ contains $x$ and $y ^ { - 1 }$ by what we have just proved, so by (2), $\pmb { H }$ also contains $x ( y ^ { - 1 } ) ^ { - 1 } = x y$ . Hence $H$ is also closed under multiplication, which proves $H$ is a subgroup of $\pmb { G }$ .
Sec. 2.1 Defi n ition and Examples

Suppose now that $H$ is finite and closed under multiplication and let $x$ be any element in H. Then there are only finitely many distinct elements among $x , x ^ { 2 } , x ^ { 3 } , \ldots$ and so $x ^ { a } = x ^ { b }$ for some integers $a , b$ with $b > a$ . If $n = b - a$ , then $x ^ { n } = 1$ so in particular every element $x \in H$ is of finite order. Then $\scriptstyle x ^ { n - 1 } = x ^ { - 1 }$ is an element of $H$ , so $H$ i s automatically also closed under inverses.

text: 2.2 CENTRALIZERS AND NORMALIZERS, STABILIZERS AND KERNELS
We now introduce some important families of subgroups of an arbitrary group $\pmb { G }$ which in particular provide many examples of subgroups. Let A be any nonempty subset of $G$ .

Definition. Define $C _ { G } ( A ) = \{ g \in G \mid g a g ^ { - 1 } = a$ for all $a \in A ]$ } . This subset of $G$ is called the centralizer of A in $G$ . Since ${ g } { a } { g } ^ { - 1 } = a$ if and only if $g a = a g$ , $C _ { G } ( A )$ is the set of elements of $G$ which commute with every element of A.

We show $C _ { G } ( A )$ is a subgroup of G. First of all, $C _ { G } ( A ) \neq \varnothing$ because $1 \in C _ { G } ( A )$ : the definition of the identity specifies that $1 a = a 1$ , for all $a \in G$ (in particular, for all ${ \pmb { a } } \in { \pmb { A } } .$ ) so 1 satisfies the defining condition for membership in $C _ { G } ( A )$ . Secondly, assume $x , y \in C _ { G } ( A )$ , that is, for all a E A, $x a x ^ { - 1 } = a$ and $y a y ^ { - 1 } = a$ (note that this does IWt mean $x y = y x $ ). Observe first that since $y a y ^ { - 1 } = a$ , multiplying both sides of this first on the left by $y ^ { - 1 }$ , then on the right by $y$ and then simplifying gives $\pmb { a } = \mathbf { y } ^ { - 1 } \pmb { a } \mathbf { y }$ , i . e . , $y ^ { - 1 } \in C _ { G } ( A )$ so that $C _ { G } ( A )$ is closed under taking inverses. Now
so $x y \in C _ { G } ( A )$ and $C _ { G } ( A )$ is closed under products, hence $C _ { G } ( A ) \leq G$ .

In the special case when $A = \{ a \}$ we shall write simply $C _ { G } ( a )$ instead of $C _ { G } ( \{ a \} )$ In this case $a ^ { n } \in C _ { G } ( a )$ for all $\mathbf { \Omega } _ { n } \in \mathbb { Z }$ .
Sec. 2.2 Centra l izers and Normal izers, Sta bil izers a nd Kernels

For example, in an abelian group $G _ { : }$ , $C _ { G } ( A ) = G ,$ for all subsets A . One can check by inspection that $C _ { Q _ { 8 } } ( i ) = \{ \pm 1 , \pm i \}$ . Some other examples are specified in the exercises.

We shall shortly discuss how to minimize the calculation of commutativities between single group elements which appears to be inherent in the computation of centralizers (and other subgroups of a similar nature).

Definition. Define $Z ( G ) = \{ g \in G \mid g x = x g$ for al $x \in G \}$ , the set of elements commuting with all the elements of $G$ . This subset of $\pmb { G }$ is called the center of $\pmb { G }$ .
Note that $Z ( G ) = C _ { G } ( G )$ , so the argument above proves $Z ( G ) \leq G$ as a special case. As an exercise, the reader may wish to prove $Z ( G )$ is a subgroup directly.

2.3 CYCLIC GROUPS AND CYCLIC SUBGROUPS

Let $G$ be any group and let $x$ be any element of $\pmb { G }$ . One way of forming a subgroup $\pmb { H }$ of $\pmb { G }$ is by letting $H$ be the set of all integer (positive, negative and zero) powers of $x$ (this guarantees closure under inverses and products at least as far as $x$ is concerned). In this section we study groups which are generated by one element.

Definition. A group $H$ is cyclic if $\pmb { H }$ can be generated by a single element, i.e. , there is some element $x \in H$ such that $H = \{ x ^ { n } \mid n \in \mathbb { Z } \}$ (where as usual the operation is multiplication).

In additive notation $H$ is cyclic if $H = \{ n x \mid n \in \mathbb { Z } \}$ . In both cases we shall write $H = \langle x \rangle$ and say $\pmb { H }$ is generated by $x$ (and $x$ is a generator of $\pmb { H }$ ). A cyclic group may have more than one generator. For example, if $H = \langle x \rangle$ , then also $\bar { H } = \bar { \langle { x } ^ { - 1 } \rangle }$ because $( x ^ { - 1 } ) ^ { n } = x ^ { - n }$ and as $\pmb { n }$ runs over all integers so does $- _ { n }$ so that

$$
\{x ^ {n} \mid n \in \mathbb {Z} \} = \{(x ^ {- 1}) ^ {n} \mid n \in \mathbb {Z} \}.
$$

We shall shortly show how to determine all generators for a given cyclic group $\pmb { H }$ . One should note that the elements of $\langle x \rangle$ are powers of $x$ (or multiples of $x ,$ , in groups written additively) and not integers. It is not necessarily true that all powers of $x$ are distinct. Also, by the laws for exponents (Exercise 1 9 in Section 1 . 1 ) cyclic groups are abelian.

Theorem 7. Let $H = \langle x \rangle$ be a cyclic group.

list_items: (1) Every subgroup of $H$ is cyclic. More precisely, if $K \leq H$ , then either $K = \{ 1 \}$ or $K = \langle \bar { x ^ { d } } \rangle$ , where $^ { d }$ is the smallest positive integer such that $x ^ { d } \in K$ .

(2) I f $| H | = \infty$ , then for any distinct nonnegative integers $\pmb { a }$ and $^ { b }$ , $\langle x ^ { a } \rangle \neq$ $\langle x ^ { b } \rangle$ . Furthermore, for every integer m, $\langle { \bar { x } } ^ { m } \rangle = \langle x ^ { | { \bar { m } } | } \rangle$ , where $| m |$ denotes the absolute value of $\mathbf { \nabla } m$ , so that the nontrivial subgroups of $H$ correspond bijectively with the integers 1 , 2, 3 , . . . .

(3) If $| H | = n < \infty$ , then for each positive integer $^ { a }$ dividing $\pmb { n }$ there is a unique subgroup of $H$ of order a . This subgroup is the cyclic group $\langle x ^ { d } \rangle$ , where $d = \frac { \mathbf { \bar { n } } } { a }$ . Furthermore, for every integer $\mathbf { \nabla } m$ , $\langle x ^ { m } \rangle = \langle x ^ { ( n , m ) } \rangle$ , so that the subgroups of $\pmb { H }$ correspond bijectively with the positive divisors of $\pmb { n }$ .

text: 2.4 SUBGROUPS GENERATED BY SUBSETS OF A GROUP

The method of forming cyclic subgroups of a given group is a special case of the general technique where one forms the subgroup generated by an arbitrary subset of a group. In the case of cyclic subgroups one takes a singleton subset $\{ x \}$ of the group $_ G$ and forms all integral powers of $x$ , which amounts to closing the set $\{ x \}$ under the group operation and the process of taking inverses. The resulting subgroup is the smallest subgroup of $_ G$ which contains the set $\{ x \}$ (smallest in the sense that if $H$ is any subgroup which contains $\{ x \}$ , then $H$ contains $\langle x \rangle )$ . Another way of saying this is that $\langle x \rangle$ is the unique minimal element of the set of subgroups of $_ G$ containing $x$ (ordered under inclusion). In this section we investigate analogues of this when $\{ x \}$ is replaced by an arbitrary subset of $_ G$ .

Throughout mathematics the following theme recurs: given an object $_ G$ (such as a group, field, vector space, etc.) and a subset A of $G _ { : }$ , is there a unique minimal subobject of $_ G$ (subgroup, subfield, subspace, etc.) which contains A and, if so, how are the elements of this subobject computed? Students may already have encountered this question in the study of vector spaces. When $_ G$ is a vector space (with, say, real number scalars) and $A = \{ v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , \ldots , v _ { n } \}$ , then there is a unique smallest subspace of
Sec. 2.4 Subgroups Generated by Subsets of a Group

$\pmb { G }$ which contains A, namely the (linear) span of $
u _ { 1 } , 
u _ { 2 } , \ldots , 
u _ { n }$ and each vector in this span can be written as $k _ { 1 } \upsilon _ { 1 } + k _ { 2 } \upsilon _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + k _ { n } \upsilon _ { n }$ , for some $k _ { 1 } , \ldots , k _ { n } \in \mathbb { R }$ When A is a single nonzero vector, v, the span of $\{  oldsymbol { \upsilon } \}$ is simply the 1-dimensional subspace or line containing v and every element of this subspace is of the form kv for some $k \in \mathbb { R }$ This is the analogue in the theory of vector spaces of cyclic subgroups of a group. Note that the 1 -dimensional subspaces contain $\pmb { k } \pmb { \upsilon }$ , where $k \in \mathbb { R } ,$ . not just $\pmb { k } \pmb { \upsilon } .$ , where $k \in \mathbb { Z } ;$ the reason being that a subspace must be closed under all the vector space operations (e.g., scalar multiplication) not just the group operation of vector addition.

Let $\pmb { G }$ be any group and let A be any subset of G. We now make precise the notion of the subgroup of $\pmb { G }$ generated by A. We prove that because the intersection of any set of subgroups of $\pmb { G }$ is also a subgroup of $\pmb { G }$ , the subgroup generated by A is the unique smallest subgroup of $\pmb { G }$ containing A; it is " smallest" in the sense of being the minimal element of the set of all subgroups containing A. We show that the elements of this subgroup are obtained by closing the given subset under the group operation (and taking inverses). In succeeding parts of the text when we develop the theory of other algebraic objects we shall refer to this section as the paradigm in proving that a given subset is contained in a unique smallest subobject and that the elements of this subobject are obtained by closing the subset under the operations which define the object. Since in the latter chapters the details will be omitted, students should acquire a solid understanding of the process at this point.
In order to proceed we need only the following.
Proposition 8. If $A$ is any nonempty collection of subgroups of $G ,$ , then the intersection of all members of $A$ is also a subgroup of $\pmb { G }$ .
Proof: This is an easy application of the subgroup criterion (see also Exercise 10, Section 1 ). Let

$$
K = \bigcap_ {H \in \mathcal {A}} H.
$$

Since each $H \in { \cal A }$ is a subgroup, $1 \in H$ , so $1 \in K$ , that is, $K \neq \emptyset .$ . If $a , b \in K$ , then $a , b \in H$ , for all $H \in A .$ . Since each $H$ is a group, $a b ^ { - 1 } \in H$ , for all $H$ , hence $a b ^ { - 1 } \in K$ . Proposition 1 gives that $K \leq G$ .
Definition. If A is any subset of the group $\pmb { G }$ define

$$
\langle A\rangle = \bigcap_{\substack{A\subseteq H\\ H\leq G}}H.
$$
This is called the subgroup of G generated by A.

Thus { A ) is the intersection of all subgroups of $\pmb { G }$ containing A. It is a subgroup of $\pmb { G }$ by Proposition 8 applied to the set $\mathcal { A } = \{ H \leq G \mid A \subseteq H \}$ $\boldsymbol { A }$ is nonempty since $G \in { \mathcal { A } } )$ . Since A lies in each $H \in { \mathcal { A } }$ A is a subset of their intersection, { A ) . Note that { A ) is the unique minimal element of $A$ as follows: { A ) is a subgroup of $\pmb { G }$ containing A, so $\langle A \rangle \in { \mathcal { A } } ;$ ; and any element of $A$ contains the intersection of all elements in $\pmb { A }$ , i.e., contains { A ) .

When A is the finite set $\{ a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \ldots , a _ { n } \}$ we write $\langle a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \ldots , a _ { n } \rangle$ for the group generated by $a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \ldots , a _ { n }$ instead of $\langle \{ a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \ldots , a _ { n } \} \rangle$ . If A and $B$ are two subsets of $\pmb { G }$ we shall write $\langle A , B \rangle$ in place of $\langle A \cup B \rangle$ .

This "top down" approach to defining ( A ) proves existence and uniqueness of the smallest subgroup of $\pmb { G }$ containing A but is not too enlightening as to how to construct the elements in it. Proposition 9. $\overline { { A } } = \langle A \rangle$ .

Proof" We first prove $\overline { { A } }$ is a subgroup. Note that ${ \overline { { A } } } \neq \emptyset$ (even if $A = \varnothing$ ). If $a , b \in { \overline { { A } } }$ with $a = a _ { 1 } ^ { \epsilon _ { 1 } } a _ { 2 } ^ { \epsilon _ { 2 } } \ldots a _ { n } ^ { \epsilon _ { n } }$ and $\boldsymbol { b } ^ { - } = b _ { 1 } ^ { \bar { \delta } _ { 1 } } b _ { 2 } ^ { \delta _ { 2 } } \dots b _ { m } ^ { \delta _ { m } }$ , then

Thus $\mathbf { \Delta } _ { a b } – 1$ is a product of elements of A raised to powers $\pm 1$ , hence $a b ^ { - 1 } \in { \overline { { A } } }$ Proposition I implies $\overline { { A } }$ is a subgroup of $\pmb { G }$ .

Since each $a \in A$ may be written $a ^ { 1 }$ , i t follows that $A \subseteq { \overline { { A } } }$ , hence $\langle A \rangle \subseteq { \overline { { A } } }$ . But ( A ) is a group containing A and, since it is closed under the group operation and the process of taking inverses, ( A ) contains each element of the form $a _ { 1 } ^ { \epsilon _ { 1 } } a _ { 2 } ^ { \epsilon _ { 2 } } \ldots a _ { n } ^ { \epsilon _ { n } }$ , that is, ${ \overline { { A } } } \subseteq \langle A \rangle$ . This completes the proof of the proposition.

We now use ( A ) in place of $\overline { { A } }$ and may take the definition of $\overline { { A } }$ as an equivalent definition of ( A ) . As noted above, in this equivalent definition of $\langle A \rangle$ , products of the form ${ \pmb a } \cdot { \pmb a }$ $\cdot a , a \cdot a \cdot a , a \cdot a ^ { - 1 }$ , etc. could have been simplified to $a ^ { 2 } , a ^ { 3 }$ , 1 , etc. respectively, so another way of writing ( A ) is

$$
\langle A \rangle = \left\{a _ {1} ^ {\alpha_ {1}} a _ {2} ^ {\alpha_ {2}} \dots a _ {n} ^ {\alpha_ {n}} \mid \text {f o r e a c h} i, \quad a _ {i} \in A, \alpha_ {i} \in \mathbb {Z}, a _ {i} \neq a _ {i + 1} \text {a n d} n \in \mathbb {Z} ^ {+} \right\}.
$$
In fact, when $A = \{ x \}$ this was our definition of ( A ) .

If $\pmb { G }$ is abelian, we could commute the ${ a } _ { i }$ 's and so collect all powers of a given generator together. For instance, if A were the finite subset $\{ a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \ldots , a _ { k } \}$ of the abelian group $G ,$ , one easily checks that

$$
\langle A \rangle = \left\{a _ {1} ^ {\alpha_ {1}} a _ {2} ^ {\alpha_ {2}} \dots a _ {k} ^ {\alpha_ {k}} \mid \alpha_ {i} \in \mathbb {Z} \text {f o r e a c h} i \right\}.
$$

If in this situation we further assume that each $\pmb { a } _ { i }$ has finite order $d _ { i }$ , for all i , then since there are exactly $d _ { i }$ distinct powers of ${ a } _ { i }$ , the total number of distinct products of the form $a _ { 1 } ^ { \alpha _ { 1 } } a _ { 2 } ^ { \alpha _ { 2 } } \dots a _ { k } ^ { \alpha _ { k } }$ is at most $d _ { 1 } d _ { 2 } \ldots d _ { k }$ that is,

$$
\left| \langle A \rangle \right| \leq d _ {1} d _ {2} \dots d _ {k}.
$$
Sec. 2.4 Subgroups Generated by Su bsets of a Group

It may happen that $a ^ { \alpha } b ^ { \beta } = a ^ { \gamma } b ^ { \delta }$ even though $\pmb { a } ^ { \alpha } \neq \pmb { a } ^ { \gamma }$ and $b ^ { \beta } \neq b ^ { \delta }$ . We shall explore exactly when this happens when we study direct products in Chapter 5.

When $\pmb { G }$ is non-abelian the situation is much more complicated. For example, let $G = D _ { 8 }$ and let r and s be the usual generators of $D _ { 8 }$ (note that the notation $D _ { 8 } = \langle r , s \rangle$ is consistent with the notation introduced in Section 1 .2). Let $a = s ,$ , let $b = r s$ and let $A = \{ a , b \}$ . Since both s and r $( = r s \cdot s )$ belong to ( a, b ) , $G = \langle a , b \rangle$ , i.e., $\pmb { G }$ is also generated by $\pmb { a }$ and $\mathbf { \delta } _ { b }$ . Both $^ { a }$ and $^ { b }$ have order 2, however $D _ { 8 }$ has order 8. This means that it is not possible to write every element of $D _ { 8 }$ in the form $a ^ { \alpha } b ^ { \beta }$ , $\alpha , \beta \in \mathbb { Z } .$ . More specifically, the product aha cannot be simplified to a product of the form $a ^ { \alpha } b ^ { \beta }$ . In fact, if $G = D _ { 2 n }$ for any $n > 2$ , and $r , s , a , b$ are defined in the same way as above, it is still true that

$$
\left| a \right| = \left| b \right| = 2, \quad D _ {2 n} = \langle a, b \rangle \quad \text {a n d} \quad \left| D _ {2 n} \right| = 2 n.
$$

This means that for large $\pmb { n }$ , long products of the form abab . . . ab cannot be further simplified. In particular, this illustrates that, unlike the abelian (or, better yet, cyclic) group case, the order of a (finite) group cannot even be bounded once we know the orders of the elements in some generating set.
Another example of this phenomenon is $S _ { n }$ :

$$
S _ {n} = \langle (1 2), (1 2 3 \dots n) \rangle .
$$
Thus $S _ { n }$ is generated by an element of order 2 together with one of order n, yet $| S _ { n } | = n$ ! (we shall prove these statements later after developing some more techniques).
One final example emphasizes the fact that if $\pmb { G }$ is non-abelian, subgroups of $\pmb { G }$ generated by more than one element of $\pmb { G }$ may be quite complicated. Let

$$
G = G L _ {2} (\mathbb {R}), \quad a = \left( \begin{array}{c c} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array} \right), \quad b = \left( \begin{array}{c c} 0 & 2 \\ 1 / 2 & 0 \end{array} \right)
$$

so $a ^ { 2 } = b ^ { 2 } = 1$ but $a b = { \binom { 1 / 2 } { 0 } } \ { \binom { 0 } { 2 } }$ . It is easy to see that ab has infinite order, so $\langle a , b \rangle$ is an infinite subgroup of $G L _ { 2 } ( \mathbb { R } )$ which is generated by two elements of order 2 .

These examples illustrate that when $| A | \ge 2$ it is difficult, in general, to compute even the order of the subgroup generated by A, let alone any other structural properties. It is therefore impractical to gather much information about subgroups of a non-abelian group created by taking random subsets A and trying to write out the elements of (or other information about) ( A ) . For certain "well chosen" subsets A, even of a nonabelian group $G ,$ , we shall be able to make both theoretical and computational use of the subgroup generated by A. One example of this might be when we want to find a subgroup of $\pmb { G }$ which contains $\langle x \rangle$ properly; we might search for some element y which commutes with $_ { x }$ (i.e., $y \in C _ { G } ( x ) )$ and form $\langle x , y \rangle$ . It is easy to check that the latter group is abelian, so its order is bounded by $| x | | y |$ . Alternatively, we might instead take y in $N _ { G } ( \langle x \rangle )$ - in this case the same order bound holds and the structure of $\langle x , y \rangle$ is again not too complicated (as we shall see in the next chapter).
The complications which arise for non-abelian groups are generally not quite as serious when we study other basic algebraic systems because of the additional algebraic structure imposed.

2.5 THE LATTICE OF SUBGROUPS OF A GROUP

In this section we describe a graph associated with a group which depicts the relationships among its subgroups. This graph, called the lattice2 of subgroups of the group, is a good way of "visualizing" a group - it certainly illuminates the structure of a group better than the group table. We shall be using lattice diagrams, or parts of them, to describe both specific groups and certain properties of general groups throughout the chapters on group theory. Moreover, the lattice of subgroups of a group will play an important role in Galois Theory.

The lattice of subgroups of a given finite group $\pmb { G }$ is constructed as follows: plot all subgroups of $\pmb { G }$ starting at the bottom with 1 , ending at the top with $\pmb { G }$ and, roughly speaking, with subgroups of larger order positioned higher on the page than those of smaller order. Draw paths upwards between subgroups using the rule that there will be a line upward from A to $\pmb { B }$ if $A \leq B$ and there are no subgroups properly between A and $B .$ . Thus if $A \le B$ there is a path (possibly many paths) upward from A to $\pmb { B }$ passing through a chain of intermediate subgroups (and a path downward from $\pmb { B }$ to A if $B \geq A )$ ). The initial positioning of the subgroups on the page, which is, a priori, somewhat arbitrary, can often (with practice) be chosen to produce a simple picture. Notice that for any pair of subgroups $H$ and $\pmb { K }$ of $\pmb { G }$ the unique smallest subgroup
2The term "lattice" has a precise mathematical meaning in terms of partially ordered sets.
Chap. 2 Subgroups

which contains both of them, namely $\langle H , K \rangle$ (called the join of $\pmb { H }$ and $\pmb { K }$ ), may be read off from the lattice as follows: trace paths upwards from $H$ and $\pmb { K }$ until a common subgroup A which contains $\pmb { H }$ and $\pmb { K }$ is reached (note that $\pmb { G }$ itself always contains all subgroups so at least one such A exists). To ensure that $A = \langle H , K \rangle$ ) make sure there is no $A _ { 1 } \leq A$ (indicated by a downward path from A to $\pmb { A } _ { 1 }$ ) with both $H$ and $\pmb { K }$ contained in $A _ { 1 }$ (otherwise replace A with $\pmb { A _ { 1 } }$ and repeat the process to see if $A _ { 1 } = \langle H , K \rangle )$ . By a symmetric process one can read off the largest subgroup of $\pmb { G }$ which is contained in both $H$ and $\pmb { K }$ , namely their intersection (which is a subgroup by Proposition 8).

There are some limitations to this process, in particular it cannot be carried out per se for infinite groups. Even for finite groups of relatively small order, lattices can be quite complicated (see the book Groups of Order $2 ^ { n }$ , $n \leq 6$ by M. Hall and J. Senior, Macmillan, 1964, for some hair-raising examples). At the end of this section we shall describe how parts of a lattice may be drawn and used even for infinite groups.

Note that isomorphic groups have the same lattices (i.e., the same directed graphs). Nonisomorphic groups may also have identical lattices (this happens for two groups of order 16 - see the following exercises). Since the lattice of subgroups is only part of the data we shall carry in our descriptors of a group, this will not be a serious drawback (indeed, it might even be useful in seeing when two nonisomorphic groups have some common properties).
Examples

Except for the cyclic groups (Example 1) we have not proved that the following lattices are correct (e.g., contain all subgroups of the given group or have the right joins and intersections). For the moment we shall take these facts as given and, as we build up more theory in the course of the text, we shall assign as exercises the proofs that these are indeed correct

In many instances in both theoretical proofs and specific examples we shall be interested only in information concerning two (or some small number of) subgroups of a given group and their interrelationships. To depict these graphically we shall draw a sublattice of the entire group lattice which contains the relevant joins and intersections. An unbroken line in such a sub lattice will not, in general, mean that there is no subgroup in between the endpoints of the line. These partial lattices for groups will also be used when we are dealing with infinite groups. Finally, given the lattice of subgroups of a group, it is relatively easy to compute normalizers and centralizers. For example, in $D _ { 8 }$ we can see that $C _ { D _ { 8 } } ( s ) = \langle s , r ^ { 2 } \rangle$ because we first calculate that $r ^ { 2 } \in C _ { D _ { 8 } } ( s )$ (see Section 2). This proves $\langle s , r ^ { 2 } \rangle \leq C _ { D _ { 8 } } ( s )$ (note that an element always belongs to its own centralizer). The only subgroups which contain $\langle s , r ^ { 2 } \rangle$ are that subgroup itself and al l o f $D _ { 8 }$ . We cannot have $C _ { D _ { 8 } } ( s ) = D _ { 8 }$ because $r$ does not commute with $\pmb { s }$ (i .e . , $r \not \in C _ { D _ { 8 } } ( s ) )$ . This leaves only the claimed possibility for $C _ { D _ { 8 } } ( s )$ .

CHAPTER 3
Quotient Gro u ps a nd Homomorph isms
3.1 DEFINITIONS AND EXAM PLES

In this chapter we introduce the notion of a quotient group of a group $\pmb { G }$ , which is another way of obtaining a "smaller" group from the group $\pmb { G }$ and, as we did with subgroups, we shall use quotient groups to study the structure of $\pmb { G }$ . The structure of the group $\pmb { G }$ is reflected in the structure of the quotient groups and the subgroups of $\pmb { G }$ . For example, we shall see that the lattice of subgroups for a quotient of $\pmb { G }$ is reflected at the "top" (in a precise sense) of the lattice for $\pmb { G }$ whereas the lattice for a subgroup of $\pmb { G }$ occurs naturally at the "bottom." One can therefore obtain information about the group $\pmb { G }$ by combining this information and we shall indicate how some classification theorems arise in this way.

The study of the quotient groups of $\pmb { G }$ is essentially equivalent to the study of the homomorphisms of $G ,$ , i.e., the maps of the group $\pmb { G }$ to another group which respect the group structures. If $\varphi$ is a homomorphism from $\pmb { G }$ to a group $\pmb { H }$ recall that the fibers of $\varphi$ are the sets of elements of $\pmb { G }$ projecting to single elements of $H$ , which we can represent pictorially in Figure 1, where the vertical line in the box above a point $\pmb { a }$ represents the fiber of $\varphi$ over $\pmb { a }$ .

The group operation in $H$ provides a way to multiply two elements in the image of $\varphi$ (i.e., two elements on the horizontal line in Figure 1). This suggests a natural multiplication of the fibers lying above these two points making the set of fibers into a group: if $X _ { a }$ is the fiber above $\pmb { a }$ and $X _ { b }$ is the fiber above $^ { b }$ then the product of $X _ { a }$ with $X _ { b }$ is defined to be the fiber $X _ { a b }$ above the product ab, i.e., $X _ { a } X _ { b } = X _ { a b }$ · This multiplication is associative since multiplication is associative in $H _ { \cdot }$ , the identity is the fiber over the identity of $H$ , and the inverse of the fiber over $\pmb { a }$ is the fiber over $\displaystyle { \pmb { a } } ^ { - 1 }$ , as is easily checked from the definition. For example, the associativity is proved as follows: $( X _ { a } X _ { b } ) X _ { c } = ( X _ { a b } ) X _ { c } = X _ { ( a b ) c }$ and $X _ { a } ( X _ { b } X _ { c } ) = X _ { a } ( X _ { b c } ) = X _ { a ( b c ) }$ · Since $( a b ) c = a ( b c )$ in $H$ , $( X _ { a } X _ { b } ) X _ { c } = X _ { a } ( X _ { b } X _ { c } )$ . Roughly speaking, the group $G$ is partitioned into pieces (the fibers) and these pieces themselves have the structure of a group, called a quotient group of $G$ (a formal definition follows the example below).

Since the multiplication of fibers is defined from the multiplication in $H _ { i }$ , by construction the quotient group with this multiplication is naturally isomorphic to the image of $G$ under the homomorphism $\varphi$ (fiber $X _ { a }$ is identified with its image $\pmb { a }$ in $H$ ).
Example

Let $G = \mathbb { Z } ,$ , let $H = Z _ { n } = \langle x \rangle$ be the cyclic group of order $\pmb { n }$ and define $\varphi : \mathbb { Z } \to Z _ { n }$ by $\varphi ( \alpha ) = x ^ { \alpha }$ . Since

$$
\varphi (a + b) = x ^ {a + b} = x ^ {a} x ^ {b} = \varphi (a) \varphi (b)
$$

it follows that $\varphi$ is a homomorphism (note that the operation in $\mathbb { Z }$ is addition and the operation in $Z _ { n }$ is multiplication). Note also that $\varphi$ is surjective. The fiber of $\varphi$ over $x ^ { a }$ is then
i.e., the fibers of $\varphi$ are precisely the residue classes modulo n. Figure 1 here becomes:
Chap. 3 Quotient Groups and Homomorph isms

The multiplication in $Z _ { n }$ is just $x ^ { a } x ^ { b } = x ^ { a + b }$ . The corresponding fibers are ${ \bar { \mathbf { a } } } , { \bar { \mathbf { b } } } ,$ , and ${ \overline { { a + b } } } ,$ so the corresponding group operation for the fibers is $\bar { a } \cdot \bar { b } = \overline { { a + b } }$ . This is just the group $\mathbb { Z } / n \mathbb { Z }$ under addition, a group isomorphic to the image of $\varphi$ (all of $Z _ { n }$ ).

The identity of this group (the fiber above the identity in $Z _ { n }$ ) consists of all the multiples of $\pmb { n }$ in $\mathbb { Z } ,$ , namely $\pmb { n } \mathbb { Z }$ , a subgroup of $\mathbb { Z }$ , and the remaining fibers are just translates, $a + n \mathbb { Z }$ , of this subgroup. The group operation can also be defined directly by taking representatives from these fibers, adding these representatives in $\mathbb { Z }$ and taking the fiber containing this sum (this was the original definition of the group $\mathbb { Z } / n \mathbb { Z } )$ . From a computational point of view computing the product of $\bar { a }$ and $\bar { b }$ by simply adding representatives $\pmb { a }$ and $^ { b }$ is much easier than first computing the image of these fibers under $\varphi$ (namely, $x ^ { \pmb { a } }$ and $x ^ { b }$ ), multiplying these in $\pmb { H }$ (obtaining $x ^ { a + b }$ ) and then taking the fiber over this product.

We can now define some terminology associated with quotient groups.
Sec. 3.1 Defi nitions and Examples

Definition. Let $\varphi : G \to H$ be a homomorphism with kernel $\pmb { K }$ . The quotient group or factor group, $G / K$ (read $\pmb { G }$ modulo $\pmb { K }$ or simply G mod $\pmb { K }$ ), is the group whose elements are the fibers of $\varphi$ with group operation defined above: namely if $X$ is the fiber above $\pmb { a }$ and $Y$ is the fiber above $^ { b }$ then the product of $X$ with Y is defined to be the fiber above the product ab.

The notation emphasizes the fact that the kernel $\pmb { K }$ is a single element in the group $G / K$ and we shall see below (Proposition 2) that, as in the case of $\mathbb { Z } / n \mathbb { Z }$ above, the other elements of $G / K$ are just the "translates" of the kernel $\pmb { K }$ . Hence we may think of $G / K$ as being obtained by collapsing or "dividing out" by $\pmb { K }$ (or more precisely, by equivalence modulo $K$ ). This explains why $G / K$ is referred to as a "quotient" group.

The definition of the quotient group $G / K$ above requires the map $\varphi$ explicitly, since the multiplication of the fibers is performed by first projecting the fibers to $\pmb { H }$ via $\varphi _ { : }$ multiplying in $\pmb { H }$ and then determining the fiber over this product. Just as for $\mathbb { Z } / n \mathbb { Z }$ above, it is also possible to define the multiplication of fibers directly in terms of representatives from the fibers. This is computationally simpler and the map $\varphi$ does not enter explicitly. We first show that the fibers of a homomorphism can be expressed in terms of the kernel of the homomorphism just as in the example above (where the kernel was $\pmb { n } \mathbb { Z }$ and the fibers were translates of the form $a + n \mathbb { Z } ,$ ).

By Theorem 3, if we are given a subgroup $\pmb { K }$ of a group $\pmb { G }$ which we know is the kernel of some homomorphism, we may define the quotient $G / K$ without recourse to the homomorphism by the multiplication $u K v K = u v K$ . This raises the question of whether it is possible to define the quotient group $G / N$ similarly for any subgroup $N$ of G. The answer is no in general since this multiplication is not in general well defined ( cf. Proposition 5 later). In fact we shall see that it is possible to define the structure of a group on the cosets of $N$ if and only if $N$ is the kernel of some homomorphism (Proposition 7). We shall also give a criterion to determine when a subgroup $N$ is such a kernel - this is the notion of a normal subgroup and we shall consider non-normal subgroups in subsequent sections.

As indicated before, the subgroups $N$ satisfying the condition in Proposition 5 for which there is a natural group structure on the quotient $G / N$ are given a name:
Sec. 3.1 Definitions and Examples

Definition. The element $g n g ^ { - 1 }$ is called the conjugate of $\textbf { \textit { n } } \in \textbf { \textit { N } }$ by g. The set $g N g ^ { - 1 } = \{ g n g ^ { - 1 } \mid n \in N \}$ is called the conjugate of $N$ by $_ { g }$ . The element $_ { g }$ is said to nonnalize $N$ if $g N g ^ { - 1 } = N$ . A subgroup $N$ of a group $\pmb { G }$ is called nonnal if every element of $\pmb { G }$ normalizes $N$ , i.e., if $g N g ^ { - 1 } = N$ for all $g \in G$ . If $N$ is a normal subgroup of $\pmb { G }$ we shall write $N \leq G$ .

Note that the structure of $\pmb { G }$ is reflected in the structure of the quotient $G / N$ when $N$ is a normal subgroup (for example, the associativity of the multiplication in $G / N$ is induced from the associativity in $\pmb { G }$ and inverses in $G / N$ are induced from inverses in G). We shall see more of the relationship of $\pmb { G }$ to its quotient $G / N$ when we consider the Isomorphism Theorems later in Section 3.
We summarize our results above as Theorem 6.
Theorem 6. Let $N$ be a subgroup of the group $G .$ . The following are equivalent:

list_items: (1) $N \leq G$
(2) $N _ { G } ( N ) = G$ (recall $N _ { G } ( N )$ is the normalizer in $\pmb { G }$ of $N$
(3) $g N = N g$ for all $g \in G$
(4) the operation on .left cosets of $N$ in $\pmb { G }$ described in Proposition 5 makes the set of left cosets into a group
(5) $g N g ^ { - 1 } \subseteq N$ for all $g \in G$

text: Proof" We have already done the hard equivalences; the others are left as exercises.

As a practical matter, one tries to minimize the computations necessary to determine whether a given subgroup $N$ is normal in a group G. In particular, one tries to avoid as much as possible the computation of all the conjugates $g n g ^ { - 1 }$ for $\pmb { n } \in N$ and $g \in G$ . For example, the elements of $N$ itself normalize $N$ since $N$ is a subgroup. Also, if one has a set of generators for $N .$ , it suffices to check that all conjugates of these generators lie in $N$ to prove that $N$ is a normal subgroup (this is because the conjugate of a product is the product of the conjugates and the conjugate of the inverse is the inverse of the conjugate) - this is Exercise 26 later. Similarly, if generators for $\pmb { G }$ are also known, then it suffices to check that these generators for $\pmb { G }$ normalize N. In particular, if generators for both $N$ and $\pmb { G }$ are known, this reduces the calculations to a small number of conjugations to check. If $N$ is a finite group then it suffices to check that the conjugates of a set of generators for $N$ by a set of generators for $\pmb { G }$ are again elements of $N$ (Exercise 29). Finally, it is often possible to prove directly that $N _ { G } ( N ) = G$ without excessive computations (some examples appear in the next section), again proving that $N$ is a normal subgroup of $\pmb { G }$ without mindlessly computing all possible conjugates $g n g ^ { - 1 }$ •
We now prove that the normal subgroups are precisely the same as the kernels of homomorphisms considered earlier.
Proposition 7. A subgroup $N$ of the group $\pmb { G }$ is normal if and only if it is the kernel of some homomorphism.

map $\varphi$ ). By (3) of Theorem 6, $N$ is then a normal subgroup. (Another direct proof of this from the definition of normality for $N$ is given in the exercises).

The homomorphism $\pi$ constructed above demonstrating the normal subgroup $N$ as the kernel of a homomorphism is given a name:

Definition. Let $N \leq G$ . The homomorphism $\pi : G \to G / N$ defined by $\pi ( g ) = g N$ is called the natural projection ( homomorphism)1 of $\pmb { G }$ onto $G / N$ . If ${ \overline { { H } } } \leq G / N$ is a subgroup of $G / N$ , the complete preimage Of $\overline { { H } }$ in $\pmb { G }$ is the preimage of $\overline { { H } }$ under the natural projection homomorphism.

The complete pre image of a subgroup of $G / N$ is a subgroup of $\pmb { G }$ ( cf. Exercise 1) which contains the subgroup $N$ since these are the elements which map to the identity $\bar { 1 } \in \overline { { H } } .$ . We shall see in the Isomorphism Theorems in Section 3 that there is a natural correspondence between the subgroups of $\pmb { G }$ that contain $N$ and the subgroups of the quotient $G / N$ .
We now have an "internal" criterion which determines precisely when a subgroup $N$ of a given group $\pmb { G }$ is the kernel of some homomorphism, namely,

We may thus think of the normalizer of a subgroup $N$ of $\pmb { G }$ as being a measure of "how close" $N$ is to being a normal subgroup (this explains the choice of name for this subgroup). Keep in mind that the property of being normal is an embedding property, that is, it depends on the relation of $N$ to $\pmb { G }$ , not on the internal structure of $N$ itself (the same group $N$ may be a normal subgroup of $\pmb { G }$ but not be normal in a larger group containing $\pmb { G }$ ) .

We began the discussion of quotient groups with the existence of a homomorphism $\varphi$ of $\pmb { G }$ to $H$ and showed the kernel of this homomorphism is a normal subgroup $N$ of $\pmb { G }$ and the quotient $G / N$ (defined in terms of fibers originally) is naturally isomorphic

1 The word "natural" has a precise mathematical meaning in the theory of categories; for our purposes we use the term to indicate that the definition of this homomorphism is a "coordinate free" projection i.e., is described only in terms of the elements themselves, not in terms of generators for $_ G$ or $N$ (cf. Appendix II).
Sec. 3.1 Definitions and Examples

to the image of $\pmb { G }$ under $\varphi$ in $H$ . Conversely, if $N \ \leq \ G ,$ , we can find a group $H$ (namely, $G / N _ { \ast }$ ) and a homomorphism $\pi : G \to H$ such that ker $\pi = N$ (namely, the natural projection). The study of homomorphic images of $\pmb { G }$ (i.e., the images of homomorphisms from $G$ into other groups) is thus equivalent to the study of quotient groups of $\pmb { G }$ and we shall use homomorphisms to produce normal subgroups and vice versa.

We developed the theory of quotient groups by way of homomorphisms rather than simply defining the notion of a normal subgroup and its associated quotient group to emphasize the fact that the elements of the quotient are subsets (the fibers or cosets of the kernel $N$ ) of the original group $\pmb { G }$ . The visualization in Figure I also emphasizes that $N$ (and its cosets) are projected (or collapsed) onto single elements in the quotient $G / N$ . Computations in the quotient group $G / N$ are performed by taking representatives from the various cosets involved.
Some examples of normal subgroups and their associated quotients follow.

text: 3.2 MORE ON COSETS AND LAGRANGE•s THEOREM

In this section we continue the study of quotient groups. Since for finite groups one of the most important invariants of a group is its order we first prove that the order of a quotient group of a finite group can be readily computed: $| { G } / N | = \frac { | { G } | } { | N | }$ In fact we derive this as a consequence of a more general result, Lagrange's Theorem (see Exercise 19, Section 1.7). This theorem is one of the most important combinatorial results in finite group theory and will be used repeatedly. After indicating some easy consequences of Lagrange's Theorem we study more subtle questions concerning cosets of non-normal subgroups.
The proof of Lagrange's Theorem is straightforward and important. It is the same line of reasoning we used in Example 3 of the preceding section to compute $| D _ { 8 } / Z ( D _ { 8 } ) |$ .

Theorem 8. (Lagrange 's Theorem) If $\pmb { G }$ is a finite group and $\pmb { H }$ is a subgroup of $\pmb { G }$ , then the order of $\pmb { H }$ divides the order of $\pmb { G }$ (i e . , $| H | | G | )$ and the number of left cosets of $\pmb { H }$ .  m $\pmb { G }$ equals $\frac { | G | } { | H | }$ .
Proof" Let $| H | = n$ and let the number of left cosets of $\pmb { H }$ in $\pmb { G }$ equal $\pmb { k }$ . By
Sec. 3.2 More on Cosets and Lagrange's Theorem
Proposition 4 the set of left cosets of $H$ in $\pmb { G }$ partition $\pmb { G }$ . By definition of a left coset the map:

$$
H \rightarrow g H \quad \text {d e f i n e d b y} \quad h \mapsto g h
$$

is a surjection from $H$ to the left coset $g H$ . The left cancellation law implies this map is injective since $g h _ { 1 } = g h _ { 2 }$ implies $\pmb { h } _ { 1 } = \pmb { h } _ { 2 }$ . This proves that $\pmb { H }$ and $g H$ have the same order:

$$
\left| g H \right| = \left| H \right| = n.
$$

Since $G$ i s partitioned into $\pmb { k }$ disjoint subsets each o f which has cardinality ${ \mathfrak { \mathbf { \ell } } } _ { 1 } , | G | = k n$ Thus $k = { \frac { \tilde { | } G | } { n } } = { \frac { | G | } { | H | } }$ , completmg the proof.
Definition. If $\pmb { G }$ is a group (possibly infinite) and $H \leq G$ , the number of left cosets of $\pmb { H }$ in $_ G$ is called the index of $H$ in $G$ and is denoted by $| G : H |$ .

In the case of finite groups the index of $\pmb { H }$ in $G$ i s $\frac { | G | } { | H | }$ For $G$ an infinite group the quotient $\frac { | G | } { | H | }$ does not make sense. Infinite groups may have subgroups of finite or infinite index (e.g., {0} is of infinite index in $\mathbb { Z }$ and $\langle n \rangle$ is of index $\pmb { n }$ in $\mathbb { Z }$ for every $\begin{array} { r } { n > 0 , } \end{array}$ ).
We now derive some easy consequences of Lagrange's Theorem.
Corollary 9. If $\pmb { G }$ is a finite group and $x \in G .$ , then the order of $x$ divides the order of $G$ . In particular $x ^ { | G | } = 1$ for all $x$ in $_ G$ .

Proof" By Proposition 2.2, $| x | = | \langle x \rangle |$ · The first part of the corollary follows from Lagrange's Theorem applied to $H = \langle x \rangle$ . The second statement is clear since now $| G |$ is a multiple of the order of $x$ .
Corollary 10. If $_ G$ is a group of prime order $\pmb { p }$ , then $\pmb { G }$ i s cyclic, hence $G \cong Z _ { p }$ .

Proof: Let $x \in G .$ , $x \neq 1$ . Thus $| \langle x \rangle | > 1$ and $| \langle x \rangle |$ divides $| G |$ . Since $| G |$ i s prime we must have $| \langle x \rangle | = | G |$ . hence $G = \langle x \rangle$ i s cyclic (with any nonidentity element $x$ as generator). Theorem 2.4 completes the proof.
With Lagrange's Theorem in hand we examine some additional examples of normal subgroups.
Examples
(1) Let $H = \langle ( 1 2 3 ) \rangle \leq S _ { 3 }$ and let $G = S _ { 3 }$ . We show $H \leq s _ { 3 }$ . As noted in Section 2.2,

$$

$$

By Lagrange's Theorem, the order of $\pmb { H }$ divides the order of $N _ { G } ( H )$ and the order of $N _ { G } ( H )$ divides the order of $\pmb { G }$ Since $\pmb { G }$ has order 6 and $H$ has order 3, the only possibilities for $N _ { G } ( H )$ are $H$ or $\pmb { G }$ . A direct computation gives

This argument illustrates that checking normality of a subgroup can often be reduced to a small number of calculations. A generalization of this example is given in the next example.

(2) Let $\pmb { G }$ be any group containing a subgroup $\pmb { H }$ of index 2. We prove $H \ \leq \ G .$ . Let $g \in G - H$ so, by hypothesis, the two left cosets of $\pmb { H }$ in $\pmb { G }$ are $1 H$ and $_ { g H }$ . Since $1 H = H$ and the cosets partition $\pmb { G }$ , we must have $g H = G - H .$ . Now the two right cosets of $\pmb { H }$ in $\pmb { G }$ are $_ { H 1 }$ and $H _ { { \pmb { g } } }$ . Since $H 1 = H$ , we again must have $H g = G - H$ . Combining these gives $g H = H g$ , so every left coset of $\pmb { H }$ i n $\pmb { G }$ is a right coset. By Theorem 6, $H \leq G$ . By definition of index, $| G / H | = 2$ , so that $G / H \cong Z _ { 2 }$ . One must be careful to appreciate that the reason $\pmb { H }$ is normal in this case is not because we can choose the same coset representatives 1 and $\pmb { g }$ for both the left and right cosets of H but that there is a type of pigeon-hole principle at work: since $1 H = H = H 1$ for any subgroup $H$ of any group $\pmb { G }$ , the index assumption forces the remaining elements to comprise the remaining coset (either left or right). We shall see that this result is itself a special case of a result we shall prove in the next chapter.

Note that this result proves that ( i ) , ( j ) and $\langle k \rangle$ are normal subgroups of $\scriptstyle Q _ { 8 }$ and that $\langle s , r ^ { 2 } \rangle , \langle r \rangle$ and $\bar { \langle } s r , r ^ { 2 } \rangle$ are normal subgroups of $D _ { 8 }$ .
(3) The property "is a normal subgroup of' is not transitive. For example,
(each subgroup is of index 2 in the next), however, $\langle s \rangle$ is not normal in ${ D _ { 8 } }$ because $r s r ^ { - 1 } = s r ^ { 2 } \not \in \langle s \rangle$ .

We now examine some examples of non-normal subgroups. Although in abelian groups every subgroup is normal, this is not the case in non-abelian groups (in some sense $Q _ { 8 }$ is the unique exception to this). In fact, there are groups $\pmb { G }$ in which the only normal subgroups are the trivial ones: 1 and G. Such groups are called simple groups (simple does not mean easy, however). Simple groups play an important role in the study of general groups and this role will be described in Section 4. For now we emphasize that not every subgroup of a group $\pmb { G }$ is normal in $G$ ; indeed, normal subgroups may be quite rare in $\pmb { G }$ . The search for normal subgroups of a given group is in general a highly nontrivial problem.
Examples

(1) Let $H = \langle ( 1 2 ) \rangle \leq S _ { 3 }$ . Since $H$ is of prime index 3 in $s _ { 3 }$ , by Lagrange's Theorem the only possibilities for $N _ { S _ { 3 } } ( H )$ are $\pmb { H }$ or $s _ { 3 }$ . Direct computation shows

Since the left coset $( 1 \ 3 ) H$ is the unique left coset of $\pmb { H }$ containing ( 1 3), th e right coset $H ( 1 \ 3 )$ cannot be a left coset (see also Exercise 6). Note also that the "group operation" on the left cosets of $\pmb { H }$ in $s _ { 3 }$ defined by multiplying representatives is not
Sec. 3.2 More on Cosets and lagra nge's Theorem

even well defined. For example, consider the product of the two left cosets $1 H$ and $( 1 3 ) H$ . The elements 1 and (1 2) are both representatives for the coset $1 H$ , yet $1 \cdot ( 1 \ 3 ) = ( 1 \ 3 )$ and $( 1 2 ) \cdot ( 1 3 ) = ( 1 3 2 )$ are not both elements of the same left coset as they should be if the product of these cosets were independent of the particular representatives chosen. This is an example of Theorem 6 which states that the cosets of a subgroup form a group only when the subgroup is a normal subgroup.

We shall see many more examples of non-normal subgroups as we develop the theory.

The full converse to Lagrange's Theorem is not true: namely, if $\pmb { G }$ is a finite group and $\pmb { n }$ divides $| G |$ , then $\pmb { G }$ need not have a subgroup of order n . For example, let A be the group of symmetries of a regular tetrahedron. By Exercise 9 of Section 1 .2, $| A | = 1 2$ . Suppose A had a subgroup $H$ of order 6. Since $\frac { | A | } { | H | } = 2 , H$ would be of index 2 in A , hence $H \leq A$ and $A / H \cong Z _ { 2 }$ . Since the quotient group has order 2, the square of every element in the quotient is the identity, so for all $g \in A ,$ , $( g H ) ^ { 2 } = 1 H _ { : }$ , that is, for all $g \in A$ , $g ^ { 2 } \in H$ . If $_ { g }$ is an element of A of order 3, we obtain $g = ( g ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } \in H$ , that is, $H$ must contain all elements of A of order 3. This is a contradiction since $| H | = 6$ but one can easily exhibit 8 rotations of a tetrahedron of order 3.

There are some partial converses to Lagrange's Theorem. For finite abelian groups the full converse of Lagrange is true, namely an abelian group has a subgroup of order n for each divisor $\pmb { n }$ of $| G |$ (in fact, this holds under weaker assumptions than "abelian"; we shall see this in Chapter 6). A partial converse which holds for arbitrary finite groups is the following result:
Chap. 3 Quotient Groups and Homomorph isms
Theorem 11. (Cauchy 's Theorem) If $\pmb { G }$ is a finite group and $\pmb { p }$ is a prime dividing $| G |$ , then $\pmb { G }$ has an element of order $\pmb { p }$ .
Proof" We shall give a proof of this in the next chapter and another elegant proof is outlined in Exercise 9.
The strongest converse to Lagrange's Theorem which applies to arbitrary finite groups is the following:

Theorem 12. (Sylow) If $\pmb { G }$ is a finite group of order $p ^ { \alpha } m$ , where $\pmb { p }$ is a prime and $\pmb { p }$ does not divide $m _ { i }$ , then $\pmb { G }$ has a subgroup of order $p ^ { \alpha }$ .
We shall prove this theorem in the next chapter and derive more information on the number of subgroups of order $p ^ { \alpha }$ .
We conclude this section with some useful results involving cosets.
Definition. Let $\pmb { H }$ and $\pmb { K }$ be subgroups of a group and define

$$
H K = \{h k \mid h \in H, k \in K \}.
$$
Proposition 13. If $\pmb { H }$ and $\pmb { K }$ are finite subgroups of a group then
Proof" Notice that H K is a union of left cosets of $\pmb { K }$ , namely,

$$
H K = \bigcup_ {h \in H} h K.
$$

Since each coset of $\pmb { K }$ has $| K |$ elements it suffices to find the number of distinct left cosets of the form $h K$ , $\textit { \textbf { h } } \in \textit { \textbf { H } }$ . But $h _ { 1 } K = h _ { 2 } K$ for $h _ { 1 } , h _ { 2 } \in H$ if and only if $h _ { 2 } ^ { - 1 } h _ { 1 } \in K .$ . Thus

$$
h _ {1} K = h _ {2} K \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad h _ {2} ^ {- 1} h _ {1} \in H \cap K \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad h _ {1} (H \cap K) = h _ {2} (H \cap K).
$$

Thus the number of distinct cosets of the form hK, for $h \in H$ is the number of distinct cosets $h ( H \cap K )$ , for $h \in H$ . The latter number, by Lagrange's Theorem, equals $\frac { | H | } { | H \cap K | }$ Thus $_ { H K }$ consists of $\frac { | H | } { | H \cap K | }$ distinct cosets of $\pmb { K }$ (each of which has $\lvert K \rvert$ . elements) which gives the formula above.

Notice that there was no assumption that $_ { H K }$ be a subgroup in Proposition 13. For example, if $G = S _ { 3 } ,$ , $H = \langle ( 1 2 ) \rangle$ and $K = \langle ( 2 3 ) \rangle$ , then $| H | = | K | = 2$ and $| H \cap K | = 1$ , so $| H K | = 4 .$ . By Lagrange's Theorem $_ { H K }$ cannot be a subgroup. As a consequence, we must have $S _ { 3 } = \langle ( 1 2 ) , ( 2 3 ) \rangle$ .

Proposition 14. If $\pmb { H }$ and $\pmb { K }$ are subgroups of a group, $_ { H K }$ is a subgroup if and only if $H K = K H$ .
Proof: Assume first that $H K = K H$ and let $a , b \in H K$ . We prove $a b ^ { - 1 } \in H K$ so H K is a subgroup by the subgroup criterion. Let

$$
a = h _ {1} k _ {1} \qquad \text {a n d} \qquad b = h _ {2} k _ {2},
$$

$$
k _ {3} h _ {3} = h _ {4} k _ {4}, \quad \text {f o r s o m e} h _ {4} \in H, \quad k _ {4} \in K.
$$
Thus $a b ^ { - 1 } = h _ { 1 } h _ { 4 } k _ { 4 }$ , and since $h _ { 1 } h _ { 4 } \in H$ , $k _ { 4 } \in K$ , we obtain $a b ^ { - 1 } \in H K$ , as desired.

Conversely, assume that $_ { H K }$ is a subgroup of $G .$ . Since $K \leq H K$ and $H \leq H K$ , by the closure property of subgroups, $K H \subseteq H K$ . To show the reverse containment let $h k \in H K$ . Since $_ { H K }$ is assumed to be a subgroup, write $h k = a ^ { - 1 }$ , for some $a \in H K$ . If $a = h _ { 1 } k _ { 1 }$ . then
completing the proof.

Note that $H K = K H$ does not imply that the elements of $\pmb { H }$ commute with those of $\pmb { K }$ (contrary to what the notation may suggest) but rather that every product hk is of the form $k ^ { \prime } h ^ { \prime }$ ( $h$ need not be $h ^ { \prime }$ nor $\pmb { k }$ be $k ^ { \prime }$ ) and conversely. For example, if $G = D _ { 2 n }$ , $H = \langle r \rangle$ and $K \ = \ \langle s \rangle$ , then $G = H K = K H$ so that $_ { H K }$ is a subgroup and $r s = s r ^ { - 1 }$ so the elements of $\pmb { H }$ do not commute with the elements of $\pmb { K }$ . This is an example of the following sufficient condition for $_ { H K }$ to be a subgroup:

Corollary 15. If $\pmb { H }$ and $\pmb { K }$ are subgroups of $\pmb { G }$ and $H \leq N _ { G } ( K )$ , then $_ { H K }$ is a subgroup of $\pmb { G }$ . In particular, if $K \leq G$ then $H K \leq G$ for any $H \leq G$ .
Proof: We prove $H K = K H$ . Let $h \in H$ , k E K . By assumption, $h k h ^ { - 1 } \in K$ , hence

$$
h k = \left(h k h ^ {- 1}\right) h \in K H.
$$
This proves $H K \subseteq K H .$ . Similarly, $k h = h ( h ^ { - 1 } k h ) \in H K$ , proving the reverse containment. The corollary follows now from the preceding proposition.
Definition. If A is any subset of $N _ { G } ( K )$ (or $C _ { G } ( K ) )$ ), we shall say A normalizes K (centralizes $\pmb { K }$ , respectively).
With this terminology, Corollary 15 states that $_ { H K }$ is a subgroup if H normalizes $\pmb { K }$ (similarly, H K is a subgroup if $\pmb { K }$ normalizes $\pmb { H }$ ).

In some instances one can prove that a finite group is a product of two of its subgroups by simply using the order formula in Proposition 13. For example, let $G = S _ { 4 }$ , $H = D _ { 8 }$ and let $K = \langle ( 1 2 3 ) \rangle$ , where we consider $D _ { 8 }$ as a subgroup of $s _ { 4 }$ by identifying each symmetry with its permutation on the 4 vertices of a square
Chap. 3 Quotient Groups and Homomorphisms

(under some fixed labelling). By Lagrange's Theorem, $H \cap K = 1$ (see Exercise 8). Proposition 13 then shows $| H K | = 2 4$ hence we must have $H K = S _ { 4 }$ . Since H K is a group, $H K = K H$ . We leave as an exercise the verification that neither $H$ nor $\pmb { K }$ normalizes the other (so Corollary 15 could not have been used to give $H K = K H )$ ).

Finally, throughout this chapter we have worked with left cosets of a subgroup. The same combinatorial results could equally well have been proved using right cosets. For normal subgroups this is trivial since left and right cosets are the same, but for nonnormal subgroups some left cosets are not right cosets (for any choice of representative) so some (simple) verifications are necessary. For example, Lagrange's Theorem gives that in a finite group G

$$
\text {t h e n u m b e r o f r i g h t c o s e t s o f t h e s u b g r o u p H i s} \frac {| G |}{| H |}.
$$

Thus in a finite group the number of left cosets of $H$ in $\pmb { G }$ equals the number of right cosets even though the left cosets are not right cosets in general. This is also true for infinite groups as Exercise 12 below shows. Thus for purely combinatorial purposes one may use either left or right cosets (but not a mixture when a partition of $\pmb { G }$ is needed). Our consistent use of left cosets is somewhat arbitrary although it will have some benefits when we discuss actions on cosets in the next chapter. Readers may encounter in some works the notation $H \backslash G$ to denote the set of right cosets of $H$ in $\pmb { G }$ .

In some papers one may also see the notation $G / H$ used to denote the set of left cosets of $H$ in $\pmb { G }$ even when $H$ is not normal in $\pmb { G }$ (in which case $G / H$ is called the coset space of left cosets of $H$ in $\pmb { G }$ ). We shall not use this notation.
